title: 1 John
author: John the apostle (same author as the Gospel and 2–3 John; no formal address)
setting: Written to a community disrupted by secessionists — former members who have left, denying that Jesus came in the flesh and claiming a superior knowledge (proto-Gnosticism). John writes with pastoral urgency.
purpose: To give Christians assurance of eternal life by setting out three tests of authentic Christian faith — belief in the incarnate Christ, love of the brothers, and righteousness of life — and to expose those who fail these tests.
themes: God is light; walking in the light
Assurance of salvation through the three tests
Love for one another as the mark of genuine faith
The incarnation — 'in the flesh' — is non-negotiable
Confession of sin and God's faithfulness to forgive
The world vs. the children of God
The love of God as the ground of our love

outline.label: God is Light — Walk in the Light

before.label: Gospel of John — also from Ephesus

after.label: 2 John and 3 John — short letters from the same elder

key_people.name: The Elder (John)
key_people.role: The author who writes as an eyewitness ('that which we have seen with our eyes, which we looked upon and have touched with our hands', 1:1) against those who have left the community

key_people.name: The Antichrists
key_people.role: Former members who 'went out from us' (2:19) denying that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh (4:2; 2 John 7) — an early docetic tendency that John addresses with his most severe language

context: 1 John was written c. AD 90-100 from Ephesus, in the wake of a community schism in which a group has left, apparently on the basis of a proto-docetic Christology that denied the full humanity of Jesus. The letter functions as a pastoral letter to those who remain, providing three tests of genuine Christian identity: doctrinal (confessing Jesus Christ come in the flesh), moral (keeping his commandments), and social (loving one another).
christ_connection: 1 John opens with the Incarnation as its theological foundation: the Word of life was from the beginning, was manifested to human senses (heard, seen, touched), and is the source of fellowship with the Father and Son (1:1-4). The denial of this full humanity is the spirit of antichrist (4:2). The atonement is described in sacrificial language: Jesus is the propitiation (hilasmos) for sins — not merely ours but those of the whole world (2:2; 4:10). This is the love: not that we loved God, but that he loved us and sent his Son as the propitiation for our sins (4:10) — the initiative and the cost are entirely God's, grounded in his character as love itself.

key_verses.note: 'If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness' — the letter's key pastoral assurance: ongoing forgiveness is available to genuinely confessing believers
'Anyone who does not love does not know God, because God is love' — the most compressed theological statement in the letter; love is not merely an attribute of God but his nature
'I write these things to you who believe in the name of the Son of God, that you may know that you have eternal life' — the stated purpose of the letter: to provide assurance to genuine believers

themes_detail.title: The Three Tests of Genuine Faith
themes_detail.text: 1 John's structure revolves around three recurring tests that genuine Christianity passes: the doctrinal test (confessing Jesus Christ come in the flesh, 2:22-23; 4:2-3); the moral test (keeping his commandments, 2:3-6; 3:4-10); and the social test (loving one another, 2:9-11; 3:11-18; 4:20-21). These tests are not presented as a works-righteousness checklist but as natural expressions of what genuine faith looks like. The false teachers failed all three: they denied Jesus' full humanity, lived in sin, and separated themselves from the community. John's pastoral purpose is not to undermine assurance but to ground genuine assurance on the right evidences.

themes_detail.title: God is Light and God is Love
themes_detail.text: The two great theological declarations of 1 John are that God is light (1:5) and God is love (4:8, 16). Neither is the other's alternative: light (holiness, truth, the exposure of sin) and love (self-giving, sacrifice, initiative toward the undeserving) are both equally fundamental to God's character. The false teachers apparently emphasised spiritual experience while neglecting holiness and brotherly love; John insists that both belong to genuine knowledge of God. Walking in the light (1:7) and loving one another (4:21) are not separate requirements but dimensions of the same relationship with the God who is both light and love.

themes_detail.title: The Spirit as the Internal Witness to Truth
themes_detail.text: The Spirit features prominently in 1 John as both the test of true teaching (4:1-6 — testing the spirits) and the internal witness to the believer (3:24; 4:13 — the Spirit given to us). Those who confess Jesus Christ come in the flesh are guided by the Spirit of God; those who deny it are guided by the spirit of the antichrist. The Spirit as internal witness (5:6-12) is part of John's threefold external testimony (water, blood, and Spirit) to Jesus. The practical implication is that genuine Christians have an internal testimony from the Spirit that confirms the external testimony of Scripture and experience.

themes_detail.title: Love as the Life of the New Community
themes_detail.text: The command to love one another occurs more times in 1 John than in any other NT book, and John grounds it in the deepest possible theology: we love because God first loved us (4:19); God is love, and the one who abides in love abides in God (4:16). But John also makes the test concrete: the person who claims to love God while hating his brother is a liar (4:20); love means laying down your life as Christ laid down his (3:16); and the practical test is whether you share material possessions with a brother in need (3:17). The Johannine community's love for one another was precisely the visible sign that distinguished it from the false teachers who had separated themselves.
